primary_diagnosis.name: Post-viral dysautonomia (Long COVID phenotype) with a postural orthostatic tachycardia (POTS) pattern — acute exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Following a viral infection, autonomic dysregulation impairs the normal reflex vasoconstriction that maintains venous return on standing. Blood pools in the dependent circulation on assuming an upright posture; to defend cerebral perfusion the heart rate rises excessively (an active-stand increment of +56 bpm here, from 72 supine to 128 standing) WITHOUT a significant fall in blood pressure — the haemodynamic signature of a postural orthostatic tachycardia (POTS) pattern rather than orthostatic hypotension. The excessive tachycardia and cerebral hypoperfusion produce palpitations, tunnel vision, pre-syncope and leg weakness that resolve on lying flat as venous return is restored. Profound fatigue reflects the broader post-viral dysautonomia. The condition is benign-but-disabling and managed longitudinally in the outpatient setting with volume expansion (fluid and sodium loading), physical counter-manoeuvres, graded autonomic rehabilitation and, if refractory, GP/specialist-initiated rate-controlling therapy (beta-blocker or ivabradine). It is NOT an acute cardiac, volume-depletion or thromboembolic emergency — those were the mimics the in-person clinician actively excluded.

differential.evidence_basis: Postural tachycardia and pre-syncope on standing that resolve on lying flat, on a post-viral background — the classic pattern.
Racing heart and near-collapse could reflect a primary arrhythmia — must be actively excluded before attributing to dysautonomia; cannot obtain an ECG via telehealth.
Presyncope and tachycardia can be volume depletion — needs the hydration screen and an orthostatic-hypotension check.
Tachycardia and pre-syncope warrant a brief PE screen (breathlessness, chest pain, hypoxia, calf symptoms) even at low prior probability.
Common cause of postural pre-syncope in young people; distinguishable by the sustained excessive tachycardia and absence of a classic vasovagal prodrome/trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should recognise a postural-intolerance pattern AND keep the serious mimics (arrhythmia, volume depletion, PE) explicitly on the list to be screened — not leap to either an emergency or a dismissal.

differential.evidence_basis: Strictly postural symptoms that resolve fully on lying flat (DI-002), no chest pain and no palpitations at rest (DI-005), able to keep fluids down and passing urine (DI-007), post-viral background (DI-006), near-collapse without true loss of consciousness (DI-004) — a benign chronic dysautonomia flare with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen.

Lowered by the strictly-postural pattern, resolution on lying flat, and absence of chest pain or resting palpitations — but a resting ECG to exclude arrhythmia cannot be done remotely, so it remains an outpatient investigation item, not an emergency.

Still taking fluids and passing urine, not vomiting (DI-007); the in-person clinician found moist mucous membranes and maintained standing blood pressure (no orthostatic hypotension) — severe volume depletion excluded.
No chest pain or breathlessness (DI-005); normal oxygen saturation and clear chest, and heart rate normalises immediately on lying flat — not the PE pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen the leading diagnosis is a benign post-viral POTS-pattern flare managed in the outpatient setting. Escalating this baseline to ED/000 because 'telehealth cannot get an ECG/orthostatic vitals' is the over-triage error this case is built to catch — the remaining ECG/autonomic testing is arranged as prompt outpatient workup, not an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Post-viral dysautonomia (Long COVID) with POTS-pattern orthostatic intolerance — exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: recognise the known benign chronic cause, arrange prompt GP review + autonomic testing / resting ECG as outpatient workup, give volume/counter-manoeuvre advice and fall precautions, and attach mandatory safety-netting for genuine deterioration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final action is recognise + refer + safety-net at T2. A resting ECG and formal autonomic (active-stand / tilt) testing are appropriate outpatient investigations; they do not need to be obtained emergently given the negative acute red-flag screen.

red_flags.description: Excessive postural tachycardia (heart racing on standing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The core POTS-pattern finding; excessive but strictly postural tachycardia. Concerning in isolation but reassuring once shown to be posture-dependent and to resolve on lying flat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If mis-read as a primary tachyarrhythmia, leads to unnecessary emergency escalation; if dismissed, misses the need for outpatient cardiac/autonomic workup.

red_flags.description: Pre-syncope with tunnel vision and leg weakness on standing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cerebral hypoperfusion on standing; drives the fall risk and warrants fall precautions, but is posture-dependent and relieved by lying flat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the fall risk could lead to a traumatic fall/head injury at home.

red_flags.description: Near-collapse event — WITHOUT true loss of consciousness or injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pre-syncope, not syncope. TRUE loss of consciousness would raise concern for a cardiac cause and change the tier — here it is explicitly absent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net means a future true syncope or fall would not trigger escalation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: No chest pain — argues against an acute coronary or PE cause. If chest pain develops it becomes a T5 (000) trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not screening for chest pain would miss a genuine cardiac emergency if it arose.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia persisting while lying completely flat / palpitations at rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heart rate normalises immediately on lying flat (72 supine) and there are no resting palpitations — this is what separates a benign postural pattern from a sustained arrhythmia. Persistent supine tachycardia >120 would be a red-flag for a primary arrhythmia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a persistent supine tachycardia would miss a genuine arrhythmia.

red_flags.description: Orthostatic hypotension / signs of shock / severe dehydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Standing blood pressure maintained (no BP drop) and moist mucous membranes on in-person exam; still tolerating fluids and passing urine — severe volume depletion and shock excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing true hypovolaemia/shock would under-treat a volume emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reasoning 'I can't get an ECG or orthostatic vitals remotely, so I must send her to ED/000.' This is the primary error the case tests: with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen (postural-only, resolves lying flat, no chest pain, no supine tachycardia, tolerating fluids), the resting ECG and autonomic testing are prompt OUTPATIENT workup, not an emergency.

Being driven by the 'severe syncopal risk', 'near-collapse' and 'urgent' triage language rather than the reassuring screen. The visceral alarm is a red herring; the discriminator is that everything is posture-dependent and resolves on lying flat.
Treating a near-collapse (caught by parent, no loss of consciousness, no injury) as a true syncopal event and escalating on that basis. True LOC would change the tier; it is explicitly absent here.

The opposite failure: dismissing it as 'just anxiety' or 'just a Long COVID flare' with pure self-care and no in-person review, no ECG/autonomic referral and no safety-netting — missing the fall risk and the small residual chance of an arrhythmic cause.
Adopting the source note's low-dose naltrexone, low-dose tadalafil or NAD+ — none are evidence-based first-line POTS therapy and none are appropriate for a telehealth AI Doctor to initiate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Active-stand / lying-and-standing postural blood pressure and heart rate (measured in-person: BP 115/75 supine → 110/75 standing, HR 72 → 128, +56 bpm) — unless the patient has a validated home BP cuff and heart-rate device
Cardiac auscultation (rhythm regularity, murmurs) — in-person exam found a regular rhythm without murmurs
Mucous-membrane hydration assessment — in-person exam found moist membranes
Resting 12-lead ECG to exclude a primary arrhythmia
Formal autonomic function testing (active-stand or head-up tilt table)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign post-viral POTS-pattern flare and manage at T2: prompt GP review (within 24–48 hours) with a resting ECG and referral for autonomic testing, immediate fluid and sodium loading plus physical counter-manoeuvres, fall precautions (shower chair, help with transfers), and MANDATORY safety-netting to 000/ED for true loss of consciousness, chest pain, palpitations persisting while lying flat (HR >120 supine), or inability to keep fluids down.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign dysautonomia pattern given the negative acute red-flag screen. The real risks are (1) OVER-action — defaulting to ED/000 because vitals/ECG cannot be obtained remotely; and (2) UNDER-action — dismissing it without arranging the ECG/autonomic workup, fall precautions or safety-netting.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Sustained heart-rate rise of +56 bpm within the first minutes of standing (72 → 128 bpm) WITHOUT an orthostatic fall in blood pressure — consistent with a POTS pattern (adult criterion ≥+30 bpm; adolescent criterion ≥+40 bpm) on the in-person active-stand measurement.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a POTS-pattern orthostatic intolerance rather than orthostatic hypotension; confirmation still requires formal outpatient autonomic testing and a resting ECG to exclude an arrhythmia.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus on POTS 2015
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: POTS is defined by a sustained heart-rate increment (≥+30 bpm adults, ≥+40 bpm adolescents) on standing without orthostatic hypotension, with chronic symptoms; first-line management is volume expansion (fluid and salt), physical counter-manoeuvres and graded exercise, with pharmacotherapy reserved for refractory cases.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on post-COVID-19 condition (Long COVID) 2023
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Post-viral dysautonomia including POTS is a recognised Long COVID phenotype managed longitudinally in primary care with multidisciplinary support; escalate only for genuine red flags.
